segments.s114-g25: همه جای جنگست میدان اوی is a verbless/nominal clause in classical Persian order: 'همه' (quantifier) + 'جای' (place) + 'جنگست' (copulative phrase meaning 'is a battlefield') followed by 'میدان اوی' (= 'his field/arena'). The possessive 'اوی' (classical form of او + ی) attaches to میدان and means 'his'.
segments.s114-g26: This clause parallels the previous: two place-nouns ('بیابان و کوه') are presented and followed by the predicate 'است بستان اوی' understood as 'are (his) garden'; 'کوهست' here serves as the predicative form 'is mountain/are mountains' before naming the possessed place 'بستان اوی' (his garden). Word order places the copula/predication before the possessor phrase.
segments.s114-g27: همه جنگ با شیر و نر اژدهاست: 'همه جنگ' = 'all the fighting' (subject), followed by the prepositional phrase 'با شیر و نر اژدها' ('with lion and male dragon'), and the predicate-marker/ending '-است' is attached to 'اژدها' as in classical Persian poetry. The sequence names the opponents (شیر, نر اژدها).
segments.s114-g28: کجا here is a locative/exclamatory 'where' linking to the previous clause; the clause literally reads 'the dragon is not freed from (از) his کفش.' The negative particle 'نا' precedes the verb 'رهاست' (a stative 'is free/loose'), forming 'نا رهاست' = 'is not freed.' Note that the preposition 'از' takes the source/limit phrase before the stative verb.
segments.s114-g29: This is a poetic list of pleasures/companions: nouns are strung together by 'و'. Note that some classical spellings/words are compressed here (e.g. بوی گل = 'scent of the rose'); the sequence functions as the direct object to the following verb. The provided lemma forms are somewhat archaic/poetic.
segments.s114-g30: نکردست is an archaic negative perfect/past construction ('has not done' / 'did not give'); it takes the following noun 'بخشش' as its object. 'ورا' = 'to him' (indirect object) precedes the subject 'کردگار' (the Creator/God), which appears at the end — classical Persian often places the subject after verb phrases for emphasis.
segments.s114-g31: همیشه (always) starts the clause; the construction 'به جنگ ... اندر است' means 'is in battle with ...' — 'به' (into/at) + 'جنگ' (battle) followed by the locative/old-fashioned 'اندر' ('in/within') and the copula 'است' at the end. The opponent نهنگ here is literal 'whale' but in epic usage can mean a huge foe.
segments.s114-g32: و‌گر ('and if / but if') introduces an alternative; the same predicative pattern repeats: 'با پلنگان به جنگ اندر است' = 'he is in battle with the leopards.' Word order again places the copula 'است' at the end, standard in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s114-g33: به گوش X آمد is an idiom meaning "reached X's ear / X heard (it)"; the prepositional phrase (به گوش زن جادو) comes before the verb آمد (simple past). زن جادو is a noun‑noun compound meaning "sorceress" (woman + magic). سرود here is the thing that "came" (the subject/thing heard) and is placed after آمد in this poetic word order.
segments.s114-g34: همان is a demonstrative meaning "that/the very" and modifies the following genitive phrases. ناله رستم is "Rostam's lament" (ناله + name in the genitive relation). و links the next genitive phrase زخم رود, where زخم ("wound/sore/strain") qualifies رود — here رود is the archaic word for a stringed instrument (not the river), so the phrase reads as a second part of the description of the song.
segments.s114-g35: بیاراست is a classical past form of آراستن (to adorn) meaning "(she) adorned"; the verb precedes the object رخ را (face + object marker را) in this poetic order. بسان بهار is a simile "like spring," modifying how the face was adorned.
segments.s114-g36: وگر چند is a concessive phrase "and if / even if (although)"; چند here functions as an adverbial degree (roughly "much/very" or "somewhat/so") qualifying زیبا. نبودش is the past negative "was not," with the clitic ش = "his/her/its" attached; نگار is the subject "the beloved." Together the clause means "even if the beloved were not (very) beautiful."
segments.s114-g37: بر رستم آمد means "she came toward/upon Rostam"—بر + proper name indicates motion toward. The participial/adjunct phrase پر از رنگ و بوی follows and describes her as "full of color and scent" (پر از = "full of"; رنگ and بوی are qualities — here رنگ likely means complexion/colouration). Verb آمد is simple past.
segments.s114-g38: بپرسید is the past tense "she asked" (classical past form). The two verbs are coordinated (و). بنشست is another past verb "she sat down." نزدیک اوی is a prepositional/locative phrase "near him" where اوی is an older clitic/pronoun referring back to Rostam.
segments.s114-g39: تهمتن is an epithet for Rostam (the mighty one) and functions as the subject. به یزدان نیایش گرفت is a classical construction meaning "took up / offered prayer to God" — گرفت here is past "took" in the sense "began/assumed" (i.e., he began prayer or offered supplication to God).
segments.s114-g40: ابر آفرینها is a poetic noun phrase — literally "cloud(s) of praises/acclamations" (ابر = cloud; آفرینها = acclamations/praises). فزایش گرفت is a verbal phrase meaning "took increase" → "increased" (past). The line thus reports that praise/acclamations rose up (metaphorically, like clouds increasing).
segments.s114-g57: یکی (here) is the indefinite 'a certain' introducing a person; گنده is an adjective 'large/huge' modifying پیری. پیری is used as a noun referring to an old person (poetic; literally 'old age' but here 'the old one'). شد is the past of شدن 'became/was', and اندر is an archaic/poetic preposition 'in/within' that takes the following noun کمند 'snare/lasso' as its object. Word order: subject phrase ('یکی گنده پیری') → verb ('شد') → locative ('اندر کمند').
segments.s114-g58: پر here is the adjectival prepositive 'full (of)' that governs a following list of nouns joined by و 'and'. آژنگ is a rare/poetic word (uncertain meaning in this context). Each و is the simple coordinating 'and'; نیرنگ means 'trick/deception', بند means 'fetter/bond', and گزند means 'harm'. The whole phrase modifies the preceding clause: 'full of ...'.
segments.s114-g59: میانش is میان + enclitic -ش ('his/it‑s middle') and means 'at his/its middle' (i.e. midriff). به خنجر uses به as an instrumental/agentive preposition 'with/by (a) dagger' and خنجر is 'dagger'. به دو نیم ('into two halves') uses به as a marker of resulting division; نیم is 'half'. کرد is the past 'did/made' used here to mean 'split' or 'cut'. The object is encoded by the enclitic -ش, so the verb needs no further object phrase.
segments.s114-g60: دل جادوان = 'the hearts of the enchanters' (دل = heart; جادوان = magicians/enchanters) — Persian allows this noun + modifier order. زو is the fused poetic form of از + او ('of/from him/it'); the tokenization gave ز and و separately, but together they mean 'because of him' or 'of him'. پر از بیم is the fixed pattern 'full of fear' (پر = full, از = of, بیم = fear). کرد here means 'caused/made' (i.e. 'filled [their hearts] with fear'). The whole clause: '[It] filled the enchanters' hearts with fear of him.'
segments.s114-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از ... گشت پرداخته is a classical phrasing meaning 'having come/from being made' — آفرین here refers to the creative act (the stem of 'to create'), گشت is a past/becoming verb 'became', and پرداخته is a past participle 'worked on / fashioned'. Together they form a subordinate temporal clause 'when (he) had been fashioned by the Creator.'
segments.s114-g2: بیاورد is a past tense verb 'he brought' (classical narrative past). گلرنگ را is the definite object (را marks the object); گلرنگ here is a plant used as a dye (safflower). ساخته is a perfect participle 'that he had made' modifying the object, so the clause means 'he brought the safflower that he had made.'
segments.s114-g3: نشست here is past 'he alighted/settled' (classical use for dismounting), followed by the prepositional phrase از بر زین 'from off the saddle' (classical double preposition از ... بر). و ره برگرفت links with and + object + verb: ره ('the road') is the object and برگرفت is the simple past 'took up / set off on.'
segments.s114-g4: خم منزل is a noun phrase 'a bend/curve of the station/stage' (خم = bend, منزل = stage/stop). جادو is the agent/topic 'magic' and اندر گرفت is a classical verbal phrase meaning 'took hold / seized (within)' — literally 'in/within it took (hold)'. The word order is typical older Persian: place/phrase + اندر + گرفت conveys that magic seized him/it at that spot.
segments.s114-g5: همی + past verb رفت is a classical progressive/continuous past 'kept going.' پویان here functions as a participial epithet or name meaning 'the swift/active one' (modifying the implicit subject). به راه دراز is a prepositional phrase 'along the long road.'
segments.s114-g6: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خورشید تابان is the noun + adjective 'the shining sun.' از فراز means 'from above / from on high.' بگشت is a past verb 'turned/rose/was turned' in this context (classical poetic past of 'to turn/return'), so the whole clause reads 'when the shining sun turned/rose from on high.'
segments.s114-g7: This is a straightforward coordinate noun phrase followed by the past verb: درخت و گیا 'trees and plants' then دید 'he saw', و connects to آب روان 'and flowing water' (آب = water, روان = flowing). Word order is noun(s) — verb — additional coordinated object.
segments.s114-g8: چنان چون بود is a classical verbless/formal construction meaning 'it was such that' or 'such was' — چنان = 'such/so', چون = 'as/that', بود = past 'was'. جای مرد جوان is the noun phrase 'the place of the young man' (جای = place, مرد جوان = young man). Together: 'Such was the place of the young man.'
segments.s114-g49: روانش is the noun روان 'soul/spirit' with the possessive enclitic -ش 'his'. گمان is a noun meaning 'thought/belief' and is the direct object of نداشت. نیایش is the noun 'prayer'. نداشت is the past negative of داشتن ('to have') here meaning 'did not have'—so the clause literally reads 'his soul did not have the thought of prayer.'
segments.s114-g50: زبانش = زبان + possessive -ش 'his tongue/speech'. توان is used as a noun 'power/ability' (not the auxiliary 'can'), ستایش 'praise' is its complement, and نداشت again is past negative 'did not have'—literally 'his tongue had no ability for praise.'
segments.s114-g51: سیه 'black/dark' is predicative and گشت is the past of گشتن 'to become/turn', so سیه گشت = 'he grew dark'. چون here functions as 'as/when', and نام یزدان 'the name of God' is the stimulus; شنید is past 'heard'—together: 'he grew dark when he heard the name of God.'
segments.s114-g52: تهمتن is the proper epithet (Tahmâtan/Rostam). سبک here functions adverbially ('lightly/without seriousness' or 'frivolously') modifying the verb; چون = 'as' and درو is the contracted classical sequence در او 'at him/him' (i.e. 'toward him'). بنگرید is the classical past form of نگریستن 'to look' ('he looked at him').
segments.s114-g53: بینداخت is the past of 'to throw/cast.' از + باد literally 'from/by the wind' functions here as a spatial/medium phrase meaning 'through the air.' خم کمند = 'the curved (خم) loop/arch of the snare (کمند)' with خم qualifying کمند. The whole clause: 'he cast the snare's curved loop into the air.'
segments.s114-g54: سر 'head' is the direct object; جادو literally 'magic' here stands by metonymy for 'the magician/enchantment' (so 'the magician's head' or 'the head of the spell'). آورد is the past 'brought/caused to come.' ناگه is an adverb 'suddenly.' ببند is the classical present/imperfective form of بستن 'to bind/close' often used jussively in epic contexts to mean '(he) bound/fastened'—so together: 'he brought the magician's head and suddenly bound it.'
segments.s114-g55: بپرسید is the past third-person form 'he inquired/asked.' و is the coordinating 'and.' گفتش = گفت + enclitic ش 'he said to him.' چه چیزی = 'what thing/what' and بگوی is the subjunctive/infinitival form related to گفتن ('to say') used here as 'what should I say' or 'what to say'—the clause reports the question 'What shall I say?'.
segments.s114-g56: بدان‌گونه 'in that way/thus' is an adverbial phrase. کت 'coat' is the noun and هست is the copula 'is' (so 'there is such a coat' or 'a coat of that kind exists'). بنمای is an imperative/optative form of نمودن/نمایان کردن 'show, reveal' and روی 'face' is its object: 'show your face.' The sequence functions as reported words: 'There is such a coat; show your face thus.'
segments.s114-g17: نشست is the simple past (3rd sing.) of نشستن 'to sit' (here 'he sat'). از بر is a poetic/archaic locative combination meaning 'by/at the edge of, beside'; it precedes the noun it governs. چشمه فرخنده‌پی is a genitival phrase 'the spring of Farrokhandeh‑pi' (فرخنده‌پی is an epithet/name modifying the spring). Word order: verb first (narrative past) then the locative phrase.
segments.s114-g18: یکی جام زر is a noun phrase 'a certain/a single cup of gold' (one golden cup) placed before the verb. دید is the past of دیدن 'he saw'. پر کرده می is a participial/compound description of the cup: پر 'full' + کرده (past participle 'made') give 'filled', and می 'wine' follows as the thing with which it is filled — together 'filled with wine'. The participial phrase modifies جام.
segments.s114-g19: ابا here functions as the verb 'to refuse/decline' (poetic usage) — 'he declined/refused'. می‌یکی is the object 'one draught / one cup of wine'. نیز 'also' links the sentence’s next action to the same subject, so the clause reads roughly 'he refused one draught of wine, and also found a tanbur.' یافت is the past of یافتن 'found'.
segments.s114-g20: بیابان is a locative noun 'in the open country / wilderness' placed at the start for emphasis. چنان 'such' modifies خانه سور 'a house of feast/banquet' (سور = feast). یافت is the past 'he found'. The whole segment says he found, in the wilderness, such a house suitable for a banquet.
segments.s114-g21: تهمتن is the name/epithet (Tahmātan) as subject. مر is the old oblique form 'me / from me' indicating the source (he took it from me). آن را is the definite object 'that' with the object marker را. به بر در is a compressed locative idiom 'to his bosom / into his arms' (به + بر + در), and گرفت is the past 'he seized/took'. Word order places the object and source before the final verb گرفت.
segments.s114-g22: بزد is the past 'he struck' (smote/played). رود here names the stringed instrument (the lute/‘rūd’); the verb+object pair means 'he struck/played the instrument'. و 'and' links the next clause. گفتارها برگرفت uses گفتارها 'the songs/utterances' (plural) and برگرفت 'took up / began to recite or sing' — literally 'he took up the words', i.e. he began the singing/recital.
segments.s114-g23: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that / who'. آواره and بد نشان are coordinated predicates: آواره 'wandering / an exile' and بد نشان 'ill‑omened / ill‑fated' (نشان 'sign/mark' combined with بد 'bad'). رستم is the name as the predicate nominative, and است is the copula 'is'. Together: 'that he is Rostam, a wanderer and ill‑omened.'
segments.s114-g24: The initial که here is causal ('for / because'). از روز شادیش is a temporal/genitive phrase 'from the day of his happiness/joy' (از = from, روز = day, شادیش = his joy). بهره غم است is idiomatic: بهره 'portion/lot' + غم 'sorrow' + است 'is' — 'his lot is sorrow.' The clause explains that his portion since the day of his joy has been sorrow.
segments.s114-g41: که is the relative conjunction 'who/that' introducing a relative clause. در دشت مازندران is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the plain of Mazandaran' (در + noun). یافت is a past-tense verb 'found' (classical/poetic past), and خوان is the object meaning 'banquet/feast'. Word order is somewhat flexible and poetic: the locative phrase precedes the verb and the object follows it.
segments.s114-g42: This is an appositive/coordination giving the contents of the feast: می‌و جام = 'wine and cup' (here the manuscript/printing joins می and the conjunction). با introduces accompaniment 'with' and governs میگسار جوان 'a young cupbearer' (noun + adjective). In classical/poetic Persian the adjective follows the noun (میگسار جوان = 'a young cupbearer').
segments.s114-g43: ندانست is the negative past 'did not know' (ن + دانست). کاو here functions as an archaic/poetic 'که او' — 'that he' (a relative/introductory pronoun) rather than the literal verb 'to dig/search' in ordinary usage. جادوی is the noun 'magic/sorcery' and ریمنست appears as a name/qualifier or an obscure adjective attached to 'magic' (the exact lexical identity is archaic/poetic).
segments.s114-g44: نهفته is an adjectival/predicate participle 'hidden' or 'latent'. به رنگ = 'in the guise/colour/appearance of' (به here marks manner/state), اندر is the archaic preposition 'in/within', and اهریمنست is a fused form meaning 'is Ahriman-like' / 'is demonic' (اهریمن + -ست 'is'). Together they mean 'hidden in the guise/appearance of a demon.'
segments.s114-g45: یکی is the indefinite 'one' / 'a certain (person)'. طاس here is used substantively as 'a bald (one)' or 'the bald man' (classical Persian often uses adjectives as nouns). می‌بر is a present/habitual or narrative-present form 'took / was taking' (poetic aspect), کفش is 'shoe', and برنهاد is the past form of 'برنهادن' meaning 'put on' or 'placed on'. Word order places the verb forms and objects in a compact, poetic sequence.
segments.s114-g46: ز (= از) is the preposition 'from / out of' (poetic form). دادار is 'the Creator' (a divine epithet). نیکی here means 'goodness / kind act' and دهش is 'a gift / act of giving' (noun). کرد یاد is an inverted verbal construction: یاد کردن = 'to remember', and the poet places کرد before یاد for metrical/poetic order — so 'کرد یاد' = 'he remembered'. The whole segment says that, recalling the Creator's goodness, he made a generous gift.
segments.s114-g47: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آواز داد is a light-verb construction 'gave a voice' = 'a voice was heard' or 'he uttered (a sound/word)'; داد here is 'gave' (past). از introduces the source 'from', and خداوند مهر means 'the Lord's kindness/compassion' or 'the Lord of kindness' (مهر = favour/kindness). The clause thus reads 'when a voice of the Lord's compassion was given/uttered.'
segments.s114-g48: دگرگونه‌تر گشت is a copular/result clause: دگرگونه‌تر 'more different' / 'changed' and گشت 'became' (past of گشتن). جادو is the subject 'the magic', and به چهر = 'in/into appearance/form' (به marking the change into a state, چهر = 'face/appearance'). The phrase means the magic 'changed into a different appearance' (i.e., its form altered).
segments.s114-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. چشم تذروان is a genitive phrase 'the eye of Tazravan' (classical Persian frequently omits an ezāfe), with چشم (eye) as head and تذروان the possessor/name. یکی چشمه is the object phrase 'a certain spring' (یکی = indefinite 'a/one') and دید is the past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'saw/beheld', so the whole line reads 'When the eye of Tazravan saw a spring...'.
segments.s114-g10: یکی introduces an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain'. جام زرین is a noun + adjective ('a golden cup') with the adjective postposed, common in poetic Persian. برو here functions as a locative/relational word 'by/near it' (an archaic/poetic usage not listed among the given lemma senses). پر نبید is 'full (پر) of wine (نبید)' with پر used as an adjective 'full (of)' placed before the noun.
segments.s114-g11: یکی again marks an indefinite item 'there was/one saw'. غرم بریان is a noun + participial adjective meaning a roasted dish ('غرم' = the dish/item, بریان = 'roasted'); و نان 'and bread' joins the second food item. از برش literally 'from/by his side' uses از 'from' with برش functioning as a locative/personal phrase here (the precise morphological analysis is archaic/poetic). The main verb is omitted (ellipsis) — the clause lists what lay nearby.
segments.s114-g12: نمکدان و ریچال lists two objects 'a salt‑cellar and a ričāl' (ریچال is an obscure/rare noun or possibly a proper name/item). گرد اندرش is a compact locative phrase: گرد 'around/about' + اندر 'in/within' + ـش (sh) the 3rd‑person suffix 'his/its', giving 'around him' or 'around it'.
segments.s114-g13: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'. جادوان بد is 'evil magicians' / 'the sorcerers' (جادوان = enchanters; بد = 'bad/evil'). خور at the start is uncertain in sense here (an archaic/poetic term whose local meaning is unclear in the provided senses), but the clause as a whole reads 'When Rostam arrived...'. رسید is the past tense 'arrived'.
segments.s114-g14: از is used causally 'because of / by', introducing the cause 'آواز او' = 'his voice'. دیو is the subject 'the demon', شد is the past of شدن 'became', and ناپدید is the predicate adjective 'vanished / gone'. Together: 'Because of his voice the demon became vanished' = 'the demon vanished at his voice.'
segments.s114-g15: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'he alighted / came down' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). از باره uses از 'from' plus باره 'load/burden' (here 'from the pack/baggage' or 'from the mount'), and زین برگرفت is 'took up the saddle' — زین (saddle) precedes the verb برگرفت (past 'took up') as direct object; برگرفت is a prefixed past verb formed from بر + گرفت.
segments.s114-g16: به introduces the target/goal 'to' or 'towards' (به غرم و بنان = 'to the roast and the bread'). اندر آمد is a classical perfective phrasing 'entered / began (upon)' — here 'began (to eat)'. بنان 'fingertips' indicates he uses his fingers; شگفت follows as a comment/adverb 'marveling' or 'in wonder', modifying the action. The clause therefore says he went to the food and began, marveling, with his fingers.

sentences.s114-s13: Every place is his battlefield; the desert and the mountain are his garden.
sentences.s114-s14: All his fighting is with lions and male dragons; where the dragon is not freed from his grasp.
sentences.s114-s15: Wine and cup and the scent of roses and the cupbearer — the Creator has not granted him any gift/benefit.
sentences.s114-s16: He is ever engaged in battle with the whale (giant); and if not, he is engaged in battle with the leopards.
sentences.s114-s17: The song came to the ear of the sorceress — that same lament of Rostam and the wound of the rud.
sentences.s114-s18: She adorned her face like spring; yet even if the beloved were not very beautiful...
sentences.s114-s19: She came up to Rostam, full of color and scent; she asked and sat down near him.
sentences.s114-s20: Tahmten offered a prayer to God; clouds (or loud) acclamations increased.
sentences.s114-s29: A great old man became caught in a snare, full of āzhang, trickery, bonds, and harm.
sentences.s114-s30: He cut him in two at the middle with a dagger; it filled the enchanters' hearts with fear of him.
sentences.s114-s1: When he had been fashioned by the Creator, he brought the safflower he had made.
sentences.s114-s2: He alighted from the saddle and took the road; at a bend of the stage, magic seized him.
sentences.s114-s3: Pūyān kept going along the long road, when the shining sun rose from on high.
sentences.s114-s4: He saw trees and plants and flowing water; such was the place of the young man.
sentences.s114-s25: His soul had no thought of prayer; his tongue had no power to praise.
sentences.s114-s26: He grew dark when he heard the name of God; Tahmatan, lightly, looked at him.
sentences.s114-s27: He flung the snare's curved loop through the air; he brought the magician's head and suddenly bound (it/him).
sentences.s114-s28: He asked and said to him, 'What shall I say?' — 'There is such a coat; show your face thus.'
sentences.s114-s9: He sat down beside the spring of the Fortunate‑foot; he saw a single golden cup filled with wine.
sentences.s114-s10: He refused a draught of wine, and also found a tanbur; in the open country he found such a house for feasting.
sentences.s114-s11: Tahmten took that from me into his arms; he struck the instrument and took up the songs.
sentences.s114-s12: for he is Rostam, a wanderer and ill‑omened; for from the day of his rejoicing his portion is sorrow.
sentences.s114-s21: who, in the plain of Mazandaran, found a feast — wine and a cup, with a young cupbearer.
sentences.s114-s22: He did not know that he was under Rimen's magic; hidden in appearance, he was demonic.
sentences.s114-s23: A certain one — a bald man — took (the bowl) and put on his shoe; remembering the Creator's goodness, he gave a charitable gift.
sentences.s114-s24: When a voice came from the Lord (a voice) of compassion, the magic took on a different appearance.
sentences.s114-s5: When the eye of Tazravan saw a spring, it saw a golden cup beside it, filled with wine.
sentences.s114-s6: Nearby lay a roasted joint and bread; a salt‑cellar and a ričāl (an obscure/uncertain vessel or item) stood around him.
sentences.s114-s7: When Rostam arrived, at his voice the demon vanished.
sentences.s114-s8: He alighted, took up the saddle, and, marveling, went to the roast and bread and began (to eat) with his fingers.
